risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients who met the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to one of two groups..." Comment: The article states that participants were randomly assigned and refers to a randomized trial, but it does not describe the method used to generate the allocation sequence (e.g., computer-generated numbers, random number table, blocks, stratification, or other explicit random method). Under Cochrane guidance, 'randomized' alone without methodological detail is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, so the risk remains unclear.

_evidence.method_quote: Patients who met the inclusion criteria were randomly assigned to one of two groups (Fig. 1Fig. 1. CONSORT diagram showing the flow of the study participants through each stage of the randomized trial.).
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomly assigned to one of two groups; the method of sequence generation is not described.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article states that patients were "randomly assigned to one of two groups" but gives no description of how allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants. No central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is reported. Comment: Because the allocation concealment method is not described, the recruiter could not be confirmed to be unable to foresee the next assignment, so this must be rated Unclear risk.

_evidence.note: The article says patients were "randomly assigned to one of two groups" but gives no description of allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Neither group was aware of the treatment that the other group was receiving. All measurements of dependent variables were obtained by another therapist who was blinded to group assignment." Comment: The article states that each group was unaware of the other group's treatment and that the outcome assessor was blinded, but it does not explicitly say that participants themselves were blinded to their own allocation or that the personnel delivering the intervention were blinded. Because the domain requires evidence about participant/personnel blinding and the intervention included subjective outcomes (pain intensity and WOMAC function), the available information is insufficient to judge low or high risk.

_evidence.blinding_quote: “Neither group was aware of the treatment that the other group was receiving. All measurements of dependent variables were obtained by another therapist who was blinded to group assignment.”

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All measurements of dependent variables were obtained by another therapist who was blinded to group assignment." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly blinded, and the measured outcomes (pain intensity by NRS, functional disability by reduced WOMAC, and quadriceps strength) were all obtained by this blinded therapist. Because the subjective outcomes were assessed by a blinded independent assessor rather than by unblinded participants or clinicians, knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence outcome measurement.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: All measurements of dependent variables were obtained by another therapist who was blinded to group assignment.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Forty-two subjects were enrolled in the study, with 21 subjects divided in each group." Comment: The CONSORT information shows only pre-randomization exclusions (6 did not satisfy inclusion criteria; 2 refused) and reports 21 randomized and 21 analysed in each arm, with 0 lost post-randomization. There is no observed incomplete outcome data for the analysed week-5 outcomes, so attrition is unlikely to bias the results.

_evidence.notes: Flow indicates 50 assessed, 6 excluded for not meeting inclusion criteria and 2 refused to participate before randomisation; 42 enrolled and 21 per group analyzed. No post-randomisation attrition described.
